paper_title: Coupled Climate Model Simulation of Holocene Cooling Events

methods.description: This method involves using the ECBilt-CLIO-VECODE global climate model to simulate climate conditions over the last 9000 years, focusing on the impact of solar and other forcings on climate dynamics.

steps.description: We present results using version 3 of the ECBilt-CLIO-VECODE global climate model, consisting of three main components describing the coupled atmosphere-ocean-vegetation system in three dimensions.
steps.searchable_summary: initialize ECBilt-CLIO-VECODE climate model with three-dimensional components

steps.description: We performed a 5-member ensemble simulation forced by time-varying forcings for the last 9000 years: orbital forcing, atmospheric trace gas concentrations, and TSI variations.
steps.searchable_summary: run ensemble simulation with time-varying forcings for 9000 years

steps.description: To reduce the short-term noise in the forcing TSI series, we applied a 55-yr running mean filter.
steps.searchable_summary: apply 55-year running mean filter to TSI series

methods.description: This method reconstructs Total Solar Irradiance (TSI) variations using solar modulation parameters and historical data to study solar influence on climate.

steps.description: TSI variations are prescribed as an anomaly of the solar constant and are based on the 14C production rate derived from the tree-ring 14C record.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate TSI variations from 14C production rate

steps.description: To correct for the non-linear relationship between solar magnetic shielding, geomagnetic dipole field intensity and 14C production rate, we used model results to infer the solar modulation function.
steps.searchable_summary: infer solar modulation function from model results

steps.description: To avoid introducing an artificial trend in our solar forcing record, we removed the long-term trend in the solar modulation parameter by subtracting a 5th order polynomial fitted to the data.
steps.searchable_summary: remove long-term trend using 5th order polynomial fit
